data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-13T01:37:52.282Z</current_datetime>

Migrate this .NET Framework code to .NET 8. Provide complete migration guidance with code and pitfalls.

```csharp
public class BackgroundProcessor
{
    private Thread _workerThread;
    private volatile bool _running;

    public void Start()
    {
        _running = true;
        _workerThread = new Thread(ProcessLoop);
        _workerThread.IsBackground = true;
        _workerThread.Start();
    }

    private void ProcessLoop()
    {
        while (_running)
        {
            try
            {
                var item = GetNextItem(); // may block
                ProcessItem(item);
            }
            catch (ThreadAbortException)
            {
                // Clean up partial work
                RollbackCurrentTransaction();
                Thread.ResetAbort(); // prevent rethrow so finally runs cleanly
            }
            finally
            {
                FlushBuffers();
            }
        }
    }

    public void Stop()
    {
        _running = false;
        if (_workerThread != null && _workerThread.IsAlive)
        {
            Thread.Sleep(2000); // give it time
            if (_workerThread.IsAlive)
                _workerThread.Abort(); // force stop
        }
    }
}
```

data.reasoningText: The user wants to migrate a .NET Framework `BackgroundProcessor` class to .NET 8. This involves several critical changes since `Thread.Abort()` and `Thread.ResetAbort()` are no longer supported in .NET 8. Let me provide a comprehensive migration.

toolCalls.name: create

data.content:
The migrated code is in `BackgroundProcessor.cs`. Here's a summary of the critical changes:

### What changed and why

| .NET Framework | .NET 8 | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `Thread` + `volatile bool` | `Task` + `CancellationTokenSource` | Cooperative cancellation is the modern pattern |
| `Thread.Abort()` | `_cts.Cancel()` | `Abort()` throws `PlatformNotSupportedException` in .NET 8 |
| `Thread.ResetAbort()` | *(removed)* | `OperationCanceledException` doesn't auto-rethrow, so no equivalent needed |
| `ThreadAbortException` catch | `OperationCanceledException` catch | Different exception type for cancellation |
| `Thread.Sleep(2000)` + Abort | `_workerTask.Wait(2s)` | Structured timeout; no force-kill fallback exists |

### Key pitfalls

1. **No force-kill exists** — `Thread.Abort()` has no replacement. If `GetNextItem()` blocks without accepting a `CancellationToken`, cancellation will hang. Every blocking call must be token-aware.

2. **`ThreadAbortException` auto-rethrows; `OperationCanceledException` does not** — remove `ResetAbort()` and add an explicit `break` to exit the loop.

3. **`catch (OperationCanceledException)`** — always guard with `when (token.IsCancellationRequested)` to avoid swallowing unrelated cancellations.

4. **Dispose the `CancellationTokenSource`** — it's `IDisposable`; leaking it leaks a kernel handle.
